props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Games, Design and Play (Macklin & Sharp) — Book Review

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Soccer works as a worked example because each element is identifiable and observable. Hopscotch shows that a rock and chalk lines mean nothing without rules and players. Burnett's framework names three conflict types: procedural, affective, and substantive. The space of possibility expands over time, as new moves like the scoop shot emerge.

props.artifactTitle: Games, Design and Play - elements and rules
artifactLines: Soccer works as a worked example because each element is identifiable and observable.

Burnett's framework names three conflict types: procedural, affective, and substantive.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Games, Design and Play (Macklin & Sharp) — Book Review belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

props.artifactTitle: Games, Design and Play - elements and rules
artifactLines: Soccer works as a worked example because each element is identifiable and observable.
Hopscotch shows that a rock and chalk lines mean nothing without rules and players.
Burnett's framework names three conflict types: procedural, affective, and substantive.
The space of possibility expands over time, as new moves like the scoop shot emerge.

beats.narration_text: The soccer and hopscotch examples convinced me that rules and players give elements their meaning, and the three conflict types intrigued me. I want to apply this to a game I know. Help me: (1) list the identifiable, observable elements of a simple game the way soccer was broken down, (2) show how the same objects become meaningless without rules, like chalk lines in hopscotch, and (3) classify a real design disagreement as procedural, affective, or substantive conflict.
